did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning model, specifically a Multires U-Net architecture (a type of convolutional neural network), to classify acute and chronic UV skin lesions. The study utilized the HAM10000 dataset and a UV dataset to train the model for detecting and categorizing skin diseases caused by UV radiation, achieving approximately 95% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and training of a deep learning model (Multires U-Net) using specific datasets (HAM10000 and a UV dataset) to classify UV skin lesions. It reports original results, including performance metrics like 95% accuracy, and compares these results against baseline models (ResNet, DenseNet, U-Net), which characterizes it as a research paper rather than a review.

title: Impact of ultraviolet radiation on human skin owing to ozone depletion
brief_summary: This study analyzes the harmful effects of ultraviolet radiation on human skin caused by ozone depletion, specifically focusing on the classification of acute and chronic UV-induced skin lesions. The researchers developed a deep learning model using the Multires U-Net architecture to detect and categorize these lesions based on intensity. The model was trained and tested using the HAM10000 dataset and a public UV dataset, with data pre-processed via adaptive histogram filters to enhance contrast and remove noise. The evaluation approach involved comparing the proposed Multires U-Net against baseline models including ResNet, DenseNet, and standard U-Net using metrics such as accuracy, recall, specificity, and Dice similarity index. The key result demonstrated that the proposed Multires U-Net achieved approximately 95% accuracy, outperforming the existing techniques in detecting UV skin lesions and classifying their chronicity.

paper_terms: acute and chronic UV skin lesions
acute and chronic inflammation of UV radiation
skin diseases raised due to UV rays
lesion_types_studied.details: The study classifies skin lesions caused by UV radiation into acute and chronic categories based on intensity using the HAM10000 dataset and a UV dataset. The model achieves approximately 95% accuracy in this classification.

Skin cancer and melanoma are mentioned in the introduction and related work sections as conditions associated with UV radiation and ozone depletion, but the specific experimental classification task focuses on 'acute and chronic UV skin lesions' rather than specific histological cancer types. The HAM10000 dataset typically contains melanoma, but the paper's stated goal is classifying UV-induced lesions (acute/chronic).
Mentioned in the introduction as an acute effect of UV radiation (wrinkles, leathery skin, liver spots, actinic keratosis, and solar elastosis).
Mentioned in the introduction as an acute effect of UV radiation.
Mentioned in the introduction and related work as an effect of UV radiation.
Mentioned in the introduction as a skin disease associated with UV radiation.

all_task_types.target: Acute and chronic UV skin lesions
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is to 'classify acute and chronic UV skin lesions based on intensity' using a Multires U-Net architecture. The model predicts discrete categories (acute vs. chronic) derived from the HAM10000 and UV datasets. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as accuracy, recall, specificity, and Dice Similarity Index (DSI), with the model achieving approximately 95% accuracy.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing 10,000 skin images, cited as Tschandl 2018. Used for training and testing the deep learning model to classify acute and chronic UV skin lesions.

use.training: Used to train the Multires U-Net model for classifying acute and chronic UV skin lesions.
use.testing: Used to test the Multires U-Net model performance.

datasets_used.name: UV Dataset (Public)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset for skin lesions, specifically identified in the text as the 'UV irradiance dataset in contiguous United States' used to measure surface UV radiation, cloud coverage, and ozone column data from 2005-2015. Also referred to generally as a 'UV dataset' in the context of skin lesion classification.

use.training: Used alongside HAM10000 to train the deep learning network for detecting skin diseases raised due to UV rays.
use.testing: Used alongside HAM10000 to test the deep learning network.
use.other: Used to measure irradiance exposure of surface UV radiation, cloud coverage, annual ozone column, and AOD500 measurement over a year (2005-2015).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the efficiency of the proposed Multires U-Net model against baseline models (ResNet, DenseNet, U-Net) in detecting acute and chronic UV skin lesions.

statistical_methods_used.method: Calculation of Accuracy, Recall, Specificity, Dice Similarity Index (DSI), and F-measure
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the classification model using True Positives (TP), False Positives (FP), True Negatives (TN), and False Negatives (FN) derived from five randomly selected data folds.

statistical_methods_used.method: Loss function optimization (Binary cross-entropy and Dice-loss)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To train the deep learning model, with Dice-loss used to address high sensitivity to imbalanced data.
To determine the mean performance metrics (Accuracy, Recall, Specificity, DSI) for the proposed and baseline models across five experimental folds.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive statistics (accuracy, recall, specificity, Dice Similarity Index) averaged over five folds and compared qualitatively against baseline models.

HAM10000 and UV Dataset.experiment: Comparative analysis of Multires U-Net against ResNet, DenseNet, and U-Net across 5 folds
HAM10000 and UV Dataset.notes: The proposed Multires U-Net achieved approximately 95% accuracy (average 0.954) in detecting acute and chronic UV skin lesions, outperforming ResNet, DenseNet, and standard U-Net across all metrics (Accuracy, Recall, Specificity, DSI) averaged over five randomly selected data folds. The UV dataset was also used to measure surface UV radiation, cloud coverage, and ozone column data from 2005-2015, showing a 0.5% increase in UV irradiance over the year.

architecture_details.structure: Symmetrical paths for dense down-sampling (left) and dense up-sampling (right) with skip connectivity channels

training_details.loss_function: Dice-loss (used due to high sensitivity of binary cross-entropy to imbalanced data)

classification_or_output_head.type: Classification of acute and chronic UV skin lesions based on intensity
classification_or_output_head.details: Binary classification output indicating lesion intensity

preprocessing_techniques: Contrast-enhanced adaptive histogram analysis (adaptive histogram equalization) to equalize the image and enhance features of skin lesions
Histogram equalization to normalize and contrast the image
Sectioning images into small blocks with contrast added based on pixel intensity value to raise contrast of surrounding regions
Adaptive histogram filters to remove noise and increase contrast
conclusions: The proposed Multires U-Net deep learning model achieved approximately 95% accuracy in detecting and classifying acute and chronic UV skin lesions, outperforming baseline models (ResNet, DenseNet, and standard U-Net) across accuracy, recall, specificity, and Dice Similarity Index metrics.

The Multires U-Net architecture, utilizing 10 dense blocks, transition blocks, and adaptive histogram filtering for preprocessing, demonstrated superior efficiency in detecting UV lesions and predicting their intensity compared to existing techniques.

The study utilized the HAM10000 dataset and a public UV irradiance dataset to train and test the model, confirming the effectiveness of the proposed network in analyzing the harmful effects of UV radiation on human skin.

The authors acknowledge limitations regarding the generalization of the dataset and suggest future work should extend the network's robustness and incorporate datasets from regions with high UV radiation risk factors.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges a limitation regarding the generalization of the dataset and suggests future work should extend the network's robustness and incorporate datasets from regions with high UV radiation risk factors.

The paper does not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests; performance was evaluated using descriptive statistics (accuracy, recall, specificity, Dice Similarity Index) averaged over five folds.
The model was implemented using MATLAB2019, which may limit reproducibility for researchers using standard deep learning frameworks like PyTorch or TensorFlow.

The study combines the HAM10000 dermatological dataset with a public UV irradiance dataset (2005-2015) for training and testing, which may introduce heterogeneity in data modalities (clinical images vs. environmental metrics).
Dice-loss was specifically chosen over binary cross-entropy to address high sensitivity to imbalanced data, indicating potential class imbalance issues in the dataset.

The paper mentions using 'five randomly selected data folds' for evaluation but does not specify the random seed or detailed splitting protocol, raising potential concerns about reproducibility of the fold selection.
